data.title: Recession Alert: Trade Wars Threaten To Derail The Global Economy
data.description:
Co-authored by Austin Rogers.

For years, the big globalists have been ripping off the United States. --President Trump.

The market's longstanding hope that Trump's tariff rhetoric is merely a negotiating tactic -- an attempt to gain leverage over trading partners in order to instate better and freer trade deals -- has been dashed lately.

Instead, the market has been waking up to the reality of Trump's fundamentally protectionist beliefs on trade, such as:

  • Foreign nations have long taken advantage of the United States, exemplified by trade deficits.
  • International trade has done untold damage to the rural working-class population (Trump's base).
  • American business executives that outsource production to foreign countries are "globalists" who have shamelessly screwed over American workers to the benefit of profit margins and shareholder enrichment.
  • Tariffs are the best tool to reverse globalism and revive domestic manufacturing, whether those tariffs are kept in place for the long run or used as leverage to coerce foreign countries and globalist companies to manufacture in the United States.

We are neither endorsing nor condemning this narrative. We are simply observing what we believe to be the ideology motivating Trump's stance on trade.

The stock market is in full panic mode as it comes to grips with Trump's manifest seriousness about this trade policy.

Across Wall Street, economic analysts at JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Moody's Analytics, and Fitch are raising the odds of a 2025 recession.

When given multiple opportunities to reassure the market that the US will avoid a recession, Trump demurred, saying he would hate to predict something like that. But he did forecast in his speech to Congress that there would be "a little disturbance" as his administration works to reshape the economy into how he would like it to be.

Left-leaning economist Larry Summers puts the odds of a 2025 recession at 50%. Some say it's even higher, while others say the odds are lower.

In what follows, we will explain why the market is panicking, why recession odds are rising, and what it all means for REIT investors.

Disruptive Trade Policy Roils The Market And Raises Recession Odds

Here's a short timeline of trade policy changes just during the month of March:

We would add to this timeline that on March 11th, the premier of Ontario, Doug Ford, announced a 25% surcharge on electricity exported to neighboring American states.

Following that, Trump promised to add an additional 25% tariff on to all Canadian steel and aluminum. Later in the day, Ford got on a call with US Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and arranged a meeting. As a result, both the electricity surcharge and the additional 25% tariff on steel and aluminum were suspended -- for now.

If the 25% tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports as well as a 20% tariff on Chinese imports are ultimately implemented, this would bring the average US tariff rate back to a level not seen since the early 1940s.

After decades of building out international supply chains, this level of tariffs would be significantly disruptive to the stock market, almost certainly leading to both corporate margin compression and consumer inflation.

Particularly disruptive would be the 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum, which analysts and industry experts have warned would have severely negative effects on producers of products that use steel and/or aluminum, such as cars, washers & dryers, and beer and soda cans.

This is important, because vastly more Americans work at companies that use steel and aluminum in their own products than at companies that produce steel and aluminum.

In fact, over ten times more Americans work in steel/aluminum-using industries than in steel/aluminum-making businesses.

While research has shown that steel and aluminum tariffs in 2018-2019 did benefit domestic producers and lead to job gains at metals manufacturers, it also shows that this raised steel and aluminum prices while leading to vastly more job losses at businesses that use steel and aluminum.

Why do higher steel and aluminum prices cause job losses in businesses that use those metals? Because higher input prices put those American producers at a disadvantage compared to their foreign competitors, who do not have to pay for higher-priced metals.

One study conducted by trade economist Lydia Cox looked at the steel tariffs imposed by former President George Bush in 2002-2003, finding that they led to 168,000 fewer jobs than would've otherwise existed in steel-using industries.

That is more lost or foregone jobs per year than the entire US workforce in steel manufacturing -- about 140,000 jobs.

Steel is also a critical input in car production, an industry that employs about 1 million Americans.

Supply chains for car manufacturers have become highly complex and intertwined across the three North American countries of Canada, the US, and Mexico, and some parts cross borders several times as part of the manufacturing process. Mexico plays a particularly critical role in carmakers' supply chains.

Tariffs on Mexico and Canada, whether limited to steel and aluminum or all-inclusive, would likely cause severe damage to domestic car manufacturers.

Hence, the poor performance of American carmakers Ford (F) and General Motors (GM) since Trump's electoral victory:

On days when tariffs seem more likely to be implemented, Ford and GM sell off sharply. They only partially recover on days when there is relief from tariffs.

In short, then, the overwhelming economic consensus is that tariffs will be overwhelmingly negative on net to both the workforce and the economy more broadly.

That is why the market is selling off and recession odds are rising.

The Economy Was Already Fragile

A point we would like to emphasize here is that the US economy was already stagnant and fragile before Trump took office.

Of course, by some measurements of the economy, it looks a bit stronger, while by others, it looks a bit weaker. But in any case, it has been mediocre, at best, in recent quarters.

The Weekly Economic Index ("WEI") is a nice way to keep tabs on the economy in a fairly up-to-date way, as it is a composite measure of 10 different daily or weekly reported indicators. These include same-store retail sales, unemployment claims, payroll tax receipts, electricity output, fuel sales, and railroad volume.

Over the last two years, there has been a gentle, upward drift in the WEI, but it has mostly been below-average to average. The average WEI reading during the 2010s was 2.25, which is right where it is at today:

So, judging by the WEI, the economy is about average after a gradual, two-year rebound.

However, by other measures, the economy has been softening the last few years.

For example, nonfarm payroll growth has slowed from the post-COVID boom times of 2022 to a little over 1% year-over-year today:

Moreover, the number of sectors still in net hiring mode has declined over the last few years. Over the last year, private sector job growth has slowed to a crawl, up 1.2% over the last year (according to ADP), while government and government-dependent sectors (education and healthcare) have accounted for the lion's share of payroll growth.

Overall hiring has slowed to below pre-pandemic levels, and so have job quits -- signifying that workers are less confident that they can quickly secure another job if they leave their current one.

In the second half of 2024 and into 2025 (so far), hiring appears to have stabilized, but it has been at a fairly soft level, hence the ~1% growth in nonfarm payroll.

However, and this point is very important, none of the above charts include the most recent data on layoffs carried out by DOGE.

These layoffs, combined with unilateral spending cuts in various discretionary parts of the federal government, could set off a chain reaction of labor market weakening. The core of government layoffs could emanate out to government contractors, and then the customers of those contractors, and so on.

If private sector hiring was strong right now, the labor market may be able to quickly absorb all of these laid off federal workers. But the recent softness in private sector hiring casts doubt on that notion.

Another area of softness in the labor market is the share of workers working multiple jobs and unable to find full-time jobs.

Not only is the number of multiple jobholders higher than its pre-pandemic level, it is the highest it has been since April 2009 -- around the trough of the Great Financial Crisis.

The latest jobs report showed that the number of part-time workers who want full-time jobs has climbed to 4.9 million, the highest number since the Spring of 2021.

Note that this trend is not that recent. Rather, the trajectory has been in place for years.

Another point highlighting the growing fragility of the economy can be found in consumer finances.

While the affluent are dramatically better off today than they were before COVID-19, a large cohort of Paycheck-to-Paycheck America is basically no better off, and perhaps even worse off. Inflation has eroded their purchasing power, job and wage growth has slowed, and interest rates on consumption (credit cards and auto loans) have become punishingly high.

Auto loan and credit card debt continue to rise at a steady pace:

And while GDP and disposable income are also rising, most of those gains are flowing to Affluent America.

Meanwhile, the paycheck-to-paycheck population is having a harder and harder time keeping up with their payments. Both auto loan and credit card delinquencies have surged back to levels typically only seen during recessions.

According to a February 2025 survey from the New York Fed, 14.6% of respondents believe they will not be able to pay their minimum required payments over the next three months. That's the highest level since April 2020 (the peak of the COVID-19 recession). Non-college-educated people under 40 are the primary group pushing up this metric.

Finally, consider one more point in support of the idea that the economy was already weak before the trade war talk began: stagnating residential investment.

Housing starts have declined from their peak level in 2022, hit during a red-hot economic rebound in the wake of the pandemic, fueled by stimulus and low-interest rates. Over the last two and a half years, residential investment has been stagnant on an inflation-adjusted basis. That is, the only thing preventing residential construction spending from falling instead of staying rangebound is input cost inflation.

Construction PPI has soared 37% since the beginning of COVID-19.

With the tariffs on Canadian lumber still on the table, housing starts and residential construction could be easily sent into a downturn later this year. And when residential construction enters a downturn, the broader economy typically follows it.

Bottom-Line

Market commentators sometimes say that bull markets don't die of old age. Some shock or another comes along to kill them. The same is true for economic expansions.

As Nick Timiraos puts it in the Wall Street Journal:

Market economies tend to settle into their own equilibrium. An increase in spending and hiring sustains still more spending and hiring until some outside event—a war, oil price shock, or large increase in borrowing costs—knocks the economy off track, creating a negative feedback loop.

Trump's tariff policy agenda could certainly act as such an "outside event," knocking the economy out of its equilibrium and into recession.

The good news is that most REITs -- and certainly most of the REITs in our portfolios -- are both recession- and tariff-resistant.

In fact, the rising threat of tariffs and recession has caused nominal GDP growth expectations to decline, which has in turn caused long-term interest rates to decline, which has in turn fueled meaningful outperformance from REITs (VNQ) so far this year against the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq index (QQQ):

We think this outperformance should continue for as long as the cloud of trade wars looms over the market, because the market rightly recognizes REITs' defensive characteristics in the face of this particular economic risk.

We will happily continue to accumulate shares in undervalued REITs for as long as the market continues to serve up opportunities to do so.

data.title: Bally's Corporation: All In... And Overleveraged
data.description:
Bally's Comeback, But Will It Deliver?

Funny how things work out, huh?

One day, you're on the brink of bankruptcy, and the next, you're sweeping up casinos in Black Hawk and adding four QC&E properties under your umbrella.

Anything can happen!

Of course, the way down is always faster—just ask the S&P, which went from all-time highs to a broad market sell-off, with the once-beloved Tech stocks leading the free fall.

Wild times!

Bally's Corporation (BALY) found itself in this whirlwind of highs and lows just over a decade ago.

Back in 2004, when Bally's Corporation was formed, the poker boom was in full swing. An underdog had just taken down the World Series of Poker, and Harrah’s—now Caesars Entertainment (CZR)—was making waves by snapping up the famous Horseshoe Casino in Vegas, broadcast rights and all.

But they decided to take a different bet.

How about horse racing?

That’s what they thought. Through BLB Investors, a joint venture between Starwood Capital Group, Kerzner International and Waterford Group, they acquired Wembley plc, a British and American racetrack operator.

After the acquisition, things changed slightly.

They rebranded as Twin River Worldwide Holdings and rolled the dice on casino operations. But then they bit off more than they could chew.

Right on the edge of the 2008 financial crisis, they doubled down, pouring around $200 million into expanding their Twin River, RI property—the same one they had picked up just two years earlier for $456 million. This was a near-fatal mistake.

They had accumulated $589 million in debt—a considerable amount for the size of Twin River at the time. They had no choice but to declare bankruptcy.

The promise? Operate its properties 24/7 and cut the greyhound racing that had been generating losses for years.

But it didn't take long for them to see a way out.

There were powerful interests that weren't about to let Twin River end up among the companies that didn't make it through the GFC.

Help didn’t just show up—it was negotiated. Rhode Island stepped in, cutting a debt restructuring deal with Twin River’s creditors.

And let’s be honest, politicians don’t do favors out of the kindness of their hearts. Twin River was a cash cow for the state, and there was no way they were letting it go under.

The next few years were all about restructuring, and Standard General—a New York hedge fund—played a big role in making it happen. It didn’t take long for them to grab a 22% stake, and before you knew it, Soo Kim, Standard’s founder, had locked in his seat as company president.

Since then, they’ve been back on the rise—and fast. Especially in recent years, they’ve been on an absolute casino-buying spree, scooping up properties left and right for the past half-decade.

Let's take a look here:

  1. Anyone keeping an eye on Black Hawk, CO knows that 2020 was a game-changer. The home of Monarch Casino (MCRI) and Ameristar saw a shake-up as Bally’s took Golden Gates, Golden Gulch, and Mardi Gras off Affinity Gaming’s hands;
  2. 2021 has been a digital year for Bally’s. They acquired SportCaller in February, Monkey Knife Fight (yes, you read that correctly. Ha) in March, Bet.Works in June and Gamesys Group in October. This last deal was the biggest one yet, coming in at around $2.7 billion and officially marking Bally’s entry into the UK digital gambling scene—the largest market in the world;
  3. In 2022, they secured the Chicago casino bid with a decisive 41-7 vote from the City Council. The following year, they opened a temporary casino at the Medinah Temple and are expected to generate at least $200 million in annual tax revenue. The permanent casino is expected to open next year;
  4. Last year, they took their UK expansion to the next level—not just on phone screens, but in brick-and-mortar as well. They acquired the Aspers Casino in Newcastle, UK and transformed it into Bally's Newcastle. This is their first property outside the United States;
  5. Don’t think I forgot about this early part of the year! In February, they completed their merger with Queen Casino & Entertainment Inc., adding four casinos (DraftKings at Casino Queen, Casino Queen Marquette, Belle of Baton Rouge, Hollywood Casino Baton Rouge) in Illinois, Iowa and Louisiana. The terms are a bit complex: Queen’s shareholders received 30.5 million shares, while Bally’s paid $18.25 per share for 22.8 million shares, funding the deal with $500 million in secured senior notes. These four properties generated approximately $57.5 million in Q4 FY 2024 alone! That’s almost 10% of Bally’s revenue in the same period. Oh, and I can’t forget to mention that there was a bid for Star Entertainment Group, an Australian gaming company, for AU$250 million.

No surprise here—while other casinos watched their sales dip during the pandemic thanks to all the restrictions, Bally’s was heading the other way, riding a wave of acquisitions that sent their numbers soaring.

Take a look at this:

They’re now way bigger than they were back when bankruptcy was knocking. Thanks to a string of acquisitions, Bally’s now operates across the following segments:

  1. Casino Operations: Bally's owns and operates fifteen casinos, one golf course, and one racetrack within the United States. Their domestic operations extend to eleven states. They also operate Bally's Newcastle in the United Kingdom;
  2. iGaming: Bally's has also become a major player in the iGaming segment over time. In addition to sports betting, they also offer digital gaming through their subsidiary, Bally's Interactive International;
  3. Licensing: Bally’s holds online sports betting licenses in eighteen US states. These licenses allow the company to secure indirect revenue streams through partnerships and market-access agreements, even outside of operating its own iGaming platforms.

Alright, so we’ve seen how much Bally’s has expanded—but has all that growth actually turned into profits?

Let’s rewind to FY 2024 and see what the numbers tell us.

A Little Tour Through the Numbers

In FY 2024, Bally’s reported sales of $2.45 billion. That’s a 0.05% year-over-year increase—pretty much flat.

That doesn’t tell us much. Let’s break it down a bit.

Looking at Gaming, Casinos and Resorts saw sales increase 5.6% year-over-year to just over $1 billion. That growth was partially offset by a $77.8 million drop associated with the closure of the Tropicana Las Vegas.

All good there.

North America Interactive—which accounts for the iGaming operations in North America—reported sales of $149.5 million, up an incredible 77% but representing just over 6% of Bally’s sales.

All good there, too.

Now it’s when we look at International Interactive that things start to get serious. They were $893.7 million, down at least 6.2% year-over-year. This suggests that the Gamesys Group acquisition is starting to feel like a drag – they are probably having trouble with competition in the UK. After all, it is the world’s largest market for online gambling.

Another explanation for this is the sale of Bally’s stake in their Asian-focused partnership. They have now transferred ownership of certain intellectual property to a trust, which will receive licensing fees under a new commercial agreement.

This has seen the Gaming segment grow by just 3% year-on-year to $2.05 billion.

And then there’s what’s left: the Non-Gaming segment. That’s where the bleeding was heaviest.

The main decline was due to lower revenues from F&B and accommodation. They fell by 13.17% year-on-year and now stand at $354.7 million.

The main culprits?

The closure of the Tropicana Las Vegas and a pullback in discretionary spending—though some peers, like Monarch, actually saw improvements in this area over the same period.

Basically, stronger domestic iGaming revenue and slight year-over-year growth in casino gaming were offset by the sale of its stake in international iGaming and lower hotel and F&B revenues.

EBIT was -$38.2 million. Bad, right? That was pretty much a repeat of FY 2023 EBIT, which was just over $2 million lower year-over-year. But that’s not all cash expenses.

Gaming and Non-Gaming expenses climbed 70 basis points year-over-year, reaching 45.8% of revenue.

But that’s just part of the story.

Bally’s Chicago is still running on tight margins with its temporary casino setup, and the closing of Tropicana Las Vegas didn’t come cheap either—demolition and closure costs totaled $59.8 million, on top of $80.1 million in accelerated depreciation from asset write-downs ahead of the teardown.

Now, on the non-cash expense side, Bally’s took a $248.9 million impairment hit. That includes $125.9 million in intangible assets, $71.6 million in goodwill, and $12.8 million in other long-lived assets within the International Interactive segment. On top of that, there was a $38.6 million write-down on gaming licenses in the Casinos & Resorts segment.

Interest expenses weren’t kind to Bally’s either, climbing 9.37% year-over-year to $310.3 million. Even though gross debt came down, new borrowings—like the $940 million financing deal with GLPI for the Bally’s Chicago project—likely carried higher costs, adding to the pressure.

On top of all of this, we saw a net loss of $567.8 million, or -$11.71 per share. Of course, normalizing this and excluding non-cash items the impact is smaller, but it is still bad. Normalized EPS was -$4.30, down from the -$3.93 we saw in FY 2023.

But of course, this will not translate into negative OCF. Add back D&A and other operating assets and voila! Operating cash flow of $114 million, down 39.5% year over year. It could be worse, right?

For investing activities, Bally’s also pulled some strings. First, there was a noticeable drop in Capex compared to FY 2023. That makes sense—Bally’s temporary Chicago casino is already up and running, and the permanent one is set to open next year. In addition, there was the sale of the stake in the iGaming operations in Asia and cash inflows from sale-leaseback transactions (Bally's Kansas City and Shreveport). All of this caused Bally's to report cash flow from investments of $97.8 million.

That is, FCF was temporarily boosted by non-recurring events and was at $211.8 million.

What did they do with this money?

This is simple to say. Basically they just deleveraged their balance sheet a bit by reducing debt by $354.5 million.

They now have $3.31 billion of long-term debt and cash of $171.2 million thanks to cash inflows from investing activities. This results in a net debt of $3.13 billion. The ratio of Net Debt to EBITDA is almost 12x!

This is very, very high.

They need to deleverage this, and fast. If a recession happens and discretionary spending is cut, Bally's will be in a very difficult situation.

I definitely wouldn't want to be in their shoes.

Forget dividends, buybacks, and any kind of distribution for now. Deleveraging should be the top priority here.

Since management has not released guidance for FY 2025, let's try to give some color on this:

  1. Sales should continue to grow in low single digits year-over-year or flat. I am talking about FY 2025, because in FY 2026 the permanent Chicago casino will open and they will see some marginal improvement in sales and margins. However, this should be offset by the decline in F&B and hotels, as well as flat UK sales revenue. Gaming within the US is not expected to show as much growth, I estimate it will continue in low single digits year-over-year;
  2. Normalized losses per share are likely to continue as they miss profitability and pay huge interest on their colossal debt. As they reduce debt, gross debt costs will tend to fall and net losses should soften somewhat;
  3. Dividends and buybacks are off the table for now;
  4. Even focusing on deleveraging, the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio should remain above 10x while they adjust profitability. And this should take some time.

Valuation: Risky Bet Here, Should We Enter?

As you can see, the fundamentals aren’t exactly a jackpot. Sure, Bally’s has grown a lot over the past five years—but that growth hasn’t translated into profitability. It’s come hand-in-hand with a mountain of debt that’s starting to look less like a growth strategy and more like a gamble that hasn’t paid off yet.

Among the casino stocks (which I cover so far), Monarch and Boyd Gaming (BYD) seem like smarter options. Even if you go into iGaming, stocks like Betsson AB (OTCPK:BTSBF) seem like much better options than Bally's.

However, a bargain is a bargain. Accel (ACEL) is a 'Buy' for me precisely because it is discounted. Why wouldn't Bally's be the same?

Since Bally's is not generating positive EPS and is not expected to do so in the next two years according to analyst consensus, we will have to use the Comps Model.

Let's focus on multiples with EV.

I say this because debt is a crucial part of Bally's story.

Bally's has an EV of $5.42 billion.

Considering the analyst consensus revenue estimate of $2.56 billion and assuming an EBITDA margin of 11%—a modest 40 basis points improvement from the prior year—we’re looking at a projected FY 2025 EBITDA of roughly $281.6 million. The EV/EBITDA ratio is approximately 19.24x.

Consider that the median for casino operator stocks is 9.56x! Peer stocks give us the feeling that Bally’s is severely overvalued. Boyd Gaming is trading at an EV/EBITDA of 7.46x and Monarch at 7.74x.

Why would Bally’s deserve to trade at this multiple that exceeds the median by 101.25%!?

Well, if you know, let me know. To me, it’s clearly overpriced.

I'll skip the EV/EBIT since Bally's doesn't generate a positive EBIT.

As for EV/S, we have the same projected sales at $2.56 billion. The EV/S ratio is 2.11x. Again, Bally's is about 81.89% above the industry median of 1.16x. However, here Bally's is similar to the EV/S of Monarch (2.67x) and Boyd (2.34x).

But this is not as significant for us.

Bally's has grown its revenues quite a bit, but this has not translated into profitability. You might imagine that because they are very efficient, Boyd and Monarch trade at a premium multiple to EV/S - since this turns into profit and is distributed among shareholders.

For Bally's, the case is different.

It generates huge revenues, but does not deliver profitability; under normal conditions, it would trade at a discounted EV/EBITDA (this would not turn into profit, and investors would not care much about top-line sales).

But note that even though it does not deliver a good P&L, Bally's still trades at an EV/S well above the industry median!

This is clearly a sign of overvaluation, even if it does not seem so when compared to Boyd or Monarch.

So yeah… not exactly a bargain. I’m going with a ‘Sell’ rating here. To me, Bally’s is worth less than $10, and that implies at least 20% downside from where it’s trading. I’d stay away from this casino stock for now. Boyd Gaming and Monarch simply offer a far better risk-reward profile at this point.

That said, there is a path forward for Bally’s.

One smart move? Sell off non-core segments, just like Light & Wonder (LNW) did back in 2021 or IGT (IGT) more recently. If they go that route and use the cash to pay down their mountain of debt, it could unlock real value for shareholders—while allowing Bally’s to double down on what matters: making their casino operations profitable.

data.title: 6 Dividend Growth Stocks I'm Buying As Tariffs Crush The Economy
data.description:
The stock market is crashing. The US dollar is cratering. Business investment is grinding to a halt. Hiring is freezing up.

And none of it is all that surprising. I have been warning about these tariffs for half a year now.

As I wrote in an article in October 2024:

While some have asserted that these are not serious proposals or that they are mere negotiating tactics, my own perception is that Trump has doubled and tripled and quadrupled down on these tariff proposals because he genuinely believes that the best thing he could do for the American economy is to substitute as much production from overseas to US manufacturers as possible.

What I did not predict is that high tariffs (which everyone agrees will cause some degree of inflation) would cause interest rates to decline. I failed to consider that, in addition to raising inflation, tariffs also reduce economic growth.

In this case, the market expects real GDP growth to decline even more than inflation increases, which translates into lower nominal GDP growth and therefore lower interest rates.

In what follows, I'll offer some commentary to explain why the market hates these tariffs so much, thoughts on stocks' reaction to them so far, and how I'm investing in the midst of this economic self-immolation.

Onward.

Return of The Tariff Man

I hate to say I told you so, but...

I have been warning for a long time that President Trump's tariff threats are not mere negotiating tactics. Trump is not a free-trader. He is a protectionist -- and has been so for decades.

To quote my recent article, “5 Stocks & 3 ETFs I'm Buying As The Specter of Recession Looms”:

All you had to do was listen to what Trump said to know that he is not a free-trader at heart.

Have you ever heard a free-trader sing so much praise for the "tariff king" William McKinley or derisively refer to transnational American corporations as "big globalists" who have been "ripping off the United States"?

It seems that President Trump is ideologically committed to what Peter Navarro and Steve Bannon call "economic nationalism."

The goal was never to negotiate even freer trade deals. Rather, the goal was and is to reverse globalization and international supply chains and to coerce producers to manufacture in the United States.

As CNBC reports, a White House official responded to a question about whether trade negotiations would be forthcoming. The response?

This is not a negotiation. This is a national emergency. Any country that thinks that they can simply make an announcement promising to lower some tariffs is ignoring the big central problem of their massive non-tariff barriers.

Trump himself said on “Liberation Day” (about as Orwellian a phrase as “Inflation Reduction Act”) that “if you want your tariff rate to be zero, then you build your product right here in America.”

Apparently, even if a foreign country dropped their tariffs to zero, the minimum tariff applied to their products sold in the US would still be 10%.

So much for “reciprocity.”

There has been a lot of ideological imputation to Trump on this subject. Instinctively bullish investors and analysts have imputed to Trump what they want and hope him to believe on trade, while ignoring or downplaying the manifold evidence to the contrary.

Now, it is clear that Trump did mean all those protectionist-sounding things he said over the last 40 years or so.

I'm not surprised, but the market sure seems to be, hence the ~5% selloff in the S&P 500 (SPY) and ~6% selloff in the Nasdaq index (QQQ) as of the end of trading on Thursday, April 3rd.

I would be remiss if I did not make space for a reminder of what is at stake here. To quote a summary of the economic benefits of free trade from the Richmond Fed:

Free trade — international commerce with minimal barriers such as tariffs or quotas — promotes economic efficiency, growth and consumer welfare by allowing countries to specialize according to their comparative advantages. By removing trade restrictions, countries benefit from greater access to a wider variety of goods at lower prices, fostering increased competition, increased innovation and improved productivity. In turn, free trade expands markets, encourages the exchange of ideas and technology, and raises living standards by enabling consumers to purchase a broader selection of goods at lower prices.

Although free trade can present challenges for certain industries or workers facing international competition, its overall effect is typically positive, enhancing global economic welfare and fostering international cooperation. Economists often describe free trade as a "win-win" for countries involved.

This is why the opening lines of an FT editorial ring so tragically true:

If it endures, Donald Trump’s decision on April 2, 2025 to enact sweeping “reciprocal” tariffs on US trade partners will go down as one of the greatest acts of self-harm in American economic history. They will wreak untold damage on households, businesses and financial markets across the world, upending a global economic order that America benefited from and helped to create.

In fact, that entire FT article is excellent and highly recommended.

Here are some other miscellaneous thoughts that I have not discussed before in previous articles:

  • Fentanyl. Illegal immigration. Tax revenue. Trade deficits. And now reciprocity. What do all these things have in common? They're all different justifications used to defend the use of tariffs. But none are the core reason President Trump is implementing them. The primary rationale behind tariffs is the populist notion that they will undo decades of globalization and lead to a manufacturing resurgence in the United States.
  • The tariffs announced on April 2nd are in no way reciprocal or even remotely so. Examples of this are numerous. The tariff rate is set at 10% on countries like Singapore, which has virtually zero tariffs on US products. It's 10% on countries like Australia, Brazil, and the UK, with whom the US consistently runs a trade surplus and who each charge the US a low-single-digit tariff on average. Europe also charges tariffs in the low-single-digits, and yet the US is going to tariff European imports at 20%.

  • VATs (value-added taxes) are not trade barriers or unfair to imports. When American companies sell their products into the European market, they pay the same VAT taxes that European producers pay when they sell products in Europe. It's essentially a sales tax, paid by both domestic and foreign producers.
  • The additional “tariff equivalents” by foreign countries assumed by the administration are arbitrary guesstimates of “currency manipulation” and other non-tariff barriers. But it is not immediately clear how the administration arrived at these numbers, and it appears as though they took a maximalist approach in their calculation.
  • Shifting auto manufacturing into the United States is not a decision companies will make lightly. Labor costs for US auto production workers average $70 an hour, compared to $40 in Canada and $6 in Mexico. Plus, it would take 3 years at a minimum to build a new greenfield factory in the US, by which time the tariffs may already be gone.
  • Slightly over 40% of revenues for S&P 500 (SPY) companies are from international markets. US exporters will be hurt by foreign retaliation and boycotts, just as they were after the 2018–2019 tariffs, as well as the Smoot-Hawley tariffs of 1930.
  • Around the world, American exporters' loss will be foreign producers' gain. For example, in the years after the trade war with China kicked off in 2018, the Chinese auto industry took off. By the end of 2024, the Chinese automaker BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) sold about as many cars as Ford (F).

  • In the tiny nation of Singapore, which has very few import restrictions, BYD has rapidly become the second-largest seller of cars, behind only Toyota.

  • Other high-exporting nations will benefit from American exporters' adversity. For example, Brazil is preparing for higher Chinese demand for its agricultural products, such as soybeans, as the Chinese seek non-US suppliers.
  • Cars are expected to rise in price by 13.5% on average as a result of 25% auto tariffs.

  • The Yale Budget Lab estimates that the new average effective tariff rate will rise from the 2.5% level where it began this year to 22.5%, the highest level since 1909. The inflationary impact on average US household is about $3,800 per year. Apparel prices are expected to rise 17%.

  • Note that the total number of tariffs announced this year bring the average effective tariff rate to a level higher than the 1930s peak in the wake of the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act.
  • This effectively amounts to the biggest tax hike since 1982, and it will affect everyone -- all consumers.
  • Trump also signed an executive order closing the “de minimis” loophole that allowed shipments of $800 or less to enter the country duty-free, which had enabled a boom in small-dollar orders from companies like Shein and Temu. This will be effective May 2nd.
  • These high tariff barriers put up by the US will embitter our trading partners to us, as I wrote about in the above-linked article, resulting in retaliatory tariffs, boycotts of US products, and closer trade relationships between other countries. For example, China, Japan, and South Korea recently began working together to collectively respond to US tariffs.
  • Rather than wrestling with just how much growth we would see this year, we are now trying to discern whether we get slow growth or recession over the next year. For what it's worth, betting markets like Kalshi are showing greater than 50% odds for a recession.

As I concluded in my above-linked article:

The president has tacitly indicated that neither stock market weakness nor even a recession would cause him to waver on his tariff policy.

I am inclined to believe him.

Therefore, I am inclined to believe the economy is headed toward recession.

Stock Market Reaction: “A Little Disturbance”

During market downturns driven largely by uncertainty, the first and biggest stocks to sell off are often the highest valued and most appreciated names. Investors would generally prefer to sell at a gain than a loss.

When stocks are getting slaughtered, investors shoot the generals first, then the officers, then everyone else.

This is the only way I can make sense of the fact that Big Tech has sold off the most in the face of the tariff threat. While the S&P is down ~11.5% from it's high, the more richly valued Nasdaq index (QQQ) is down almost 16% and the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) is down over 20%.

While Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) have fared the best so far, in part due to their relatively reasonable valuations, Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA) have fared the worst:

I think the selloff in Tesla is justified. The company just reported that Q1 2025 deliveries dropped 13% year-over-year, despite rolling out a refreshed Model Y during the quarter. I think this is only the beginning of pain for Tesla. Boycotts of the car brand have reached fever pitch levels in Europe and Canada, and I think they are likely to continue and probably get worse.

On the plus side for Tesla, their manufacturing for US-sold cars is the most heavily based in the US of any carmaker.

On the other hand, Elon Musk has expressed his commitment to continuing his involvement with DOGE, which may exacerbate the worldwide boycott of Tesla.

On the other hand, consider the sectors of the stock market that were most cheaply valued, chief among them real estate.

The Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) began the year as one of the cheapest sectors of the market, having been marked down as interest rates rose. And so far this year, it has held up far better than the market, especially the real estate stocks in the S&P 500 (XLRE):

Going forward, now that there is more clarity on how and where the pain of tariffs will be distributed, I would expect the relative performance of tech to improve against other, more vulnerable sectors.

I am thinking in particular about small-cap stocks such as those in the Russell 2000 (IWM):

Unlike larger companies with greater pricing power, smaller businesses and retailers will likely have a harder time shouldering the burden of the tariffs.

How I'm Investing Right Now

First I'll say what I'm not doing right now. I'm not raising cash.

I have a very, very small position in the Vanguard Ultra-Short Term Bond ETF (VUSB), which owns bonds and commercial paper with maturities over the next several months. The ETF currently yields 4.6%, which is quite nice for a cash equivalent, but the time to buy VUSB was a month ago.

Now that many stocks are down 10-20% (or more) from their highs, the time to accumulate cash has passed, in my opinion.

During downturns like this, I try to stick to a few simple rules:

  1. Always be buying something.
  2. When stocks are crashing, focus on quality.
  3. Don't panic-sell. Only sell when I know the investment thesis is broken.
  4. Measure success by multi-year cash flow and dividend growth, not short-term stock price performance.

With that said, here are the stocks in which I'm planning to buy shares this week:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth (Guesstimate)
Blackstone (BX) ~3.5% Variable (Avg. High-Single-Digit)
Cullen/Frost Bankers (CFR) 3.4% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Carlisle Companies (CSL) 1.2% Mid-Teens
Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) 0.5% Double-Digit
Innovative Industrial Properties Preferred Series A (IIPR.PR.A) 9.4% N/A
Public Storage (PSA) 4.1% Mid- to High-Single-Digit

As I've explained over the last few weeks, I view BX as the industry leader in alternative asset management, and I don't think their competitive advantage is going away anytime soon. Investor bearishness could absolutely hinder fundraising and earnings growth for a little while, and the tariffs will almost certainly do some damage to some of their owned assets. But in the long run, BX's growth trajectory should remain intact.

I sold out of CFR in November 2024 after a post-election pop to around $140 that did not seem justified to me. I broke my own rule about only selling when the investment thesis is broken. (What can I say? I'm an ordinary sinner.) After the post-Liberation selloff, CFR looks attractive again. The Texas-focused regional bank is among the most conservatively managed in the country, it boasts a 31-year dividend growth record, and it enjoys a strong balance sheet and A- credit rating.

As explained last week, CSL is a building materials company that specializes in envelope/weather-proofing materials. Its products are used in both residential and commercial construction, as well as both new-builds and remodels.

I think the tariffs could cause a slowdown in construction for a little while, but 2026 and 2027 should offer up strong growth for CSL as construction recovers for both residential and commercial buildings.

GOOGL is by far my favorite of the Mag 7. Many investors seem to be concerned that AI chatbots will rapidly take market share from Google Search in the coming years, while Gemini only claims a portion of AI queries. That's possible, but I think Google Search is entrenched in society and won't be rapidly replaced by any AI chatbot.

Plus, investors who focus too narrowly on Search are overlooking GOOGL's many other peer-leading assets such as Waymo and YouTube, which recently captured more viewership hours on televisions (not computers or smartphones) than any other platform.

One analyst recently surmised that YouTube could be worth $550 billion, which amounts to about 30% of GOOGL's current market cap.

30%!!

It may very well be that YouTube is someday GOOGL's primary cash cow instead of Search.

In any case, AI is not about to supplant most of GOOGL's businesses, even if it does begin to eat into Search market share.

Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) owns cannabis-related real estate, mostly cultivation facilities, and has suffered some setbacks as the whole legal marijuana industry languishes right now. High capital costs have increased operators' cash burn rates and ultimately led to some lease defaults. IIPR's dividend looks unsustainable and likely to be cut over the next few months, but the preferred stock looks very attractive.

IIPR.PR.A makeup only a tiny fraction of IIPR's total capitalization, and the company has very little debt that is senior to the pref. It yields 9% at par but about 9.4% at its current share price. That yield plus a little bit of upside to par value strikes me as a very appealing risk-reward proposition.

Finally, PSA is the industry leader in self-storage. Its scale, operating efficiency, and balance sheet strength are unparalleled. And I believe that if mortgage rates decline enough, there could be a revival of housing mobility and home sales, which are generally good for self-storage real estate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 8% (April 2025)
data.description:
Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes and are presented in "italics." This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50". Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

Introduction and Market Summary:

The market has been going through a little bit of turmoil, accompanied by a sharp rise in volatility. At one point, the S&P 500 entered into the correction territory; however, it has recovered somewhat since then. There are many unknowns for the market at the moment. There are fears of a slowdown in the economy and persistent inflation. The new Trump administration is reshaping the international trade policies with new tariffs on almost every country, in addition to sector-specific tariffs. Investors and consumers alike are not sure of their near to mid-term impacts, even if some aspects may be good in the long term. On a 5-10-year horizon, tariffs may result in some good outcomes, helping to re-shore some of the manufacturing and narrow down the trade imbalances, but they are likely to cause some hardships and inflationary pressures in the short to mid-term. Also, the ongoing effort to shrink the federal government is likely a good thing, but we cannot rule out some pain in the short term. The market fears that all this uncertainty may cause consumer spending to go down and businesses to pull back from investments and hiring. Moreover, the markets were already overvalued to begin with, especially in the tech sector, due to all the AI-related hype, and needed to pull back. This is also evident from the fact that blue-chip dividend stocks have held better during this correction, as they were not very expensive to start with. There seems to be some rotation of money occurring away from high-flying stocks and into blue chip and dividend-paying stocks, and even in foreign stocks.

To make things worse, the Fed is holding interest rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50%. So far, they have signaled only two rate cuts in all of 2025. The relatively high interest rates are causing a lot of problems in many sectors of the economy, especially housing. We think the Fed needs to be flexible and ready to compensate for the additional burden that consumers may face from the new tariff regime. However, there is a risk of the Fed being too slow in cutting the rates, just as they were late in raising in 2022.

You can see the odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

It is hard to predict where the markets are going to go from here, at least until there is some clarity on some of these issues.

It is always good to have some idea of where the markets and the broader economy may be headed. Even so, we believe markets are always unpredictable, and it is not possible to catch the exact bottom (or the peak). So, it is best to invest regularly and consistently in good, solid dividend-paying stocks when their valuations are reasonable or attractive.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 3.55%, 5.54%, and 7.58%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process

Goals and Objectives:

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and are offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that would grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.

Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to roughly 700 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From thereon, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Notes: 1) Please note that when we use the term "safe" in relation to stocks and investments, it should be interpreted as "relatively safe" because nothing is absolutely safe in investing. Even though we present only 5 to 10 stocks in our final list, one should have 15-20 stocks at a minimum in a well-diversified portfolio.

2) All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and CCC-List (Dividend Radar).

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $8 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high quality but lower yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we got around 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend growth history. We cross-check our current list of over 600 stocks against the list of so-called Dividend Champions, Contenders, and Challengers originally defined and created by David Fish. Generally, the stocks with more than 25 years of dividend increases are called dividend Champions, while stocks with more than ten but less than 25 years of dividend increases are termed Contenders. Further, stocks with more than five but less than ten years of dividend increases are called Challengers. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

After we apply all the above criteria, we're left with roughly 400 companies on our list. However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling. So, by relaxing some of the conditions, roughly 60-80 additional companies were considered to be on our list. We call them category 'B' companies. After including them, we end up with roughly 450 plus companies that make up our first list.

We then import the various data elements from many sources and assigned weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield: Indicates the yield based on the current price.
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth): This provides information on how many years a company has paid and increased dividends on a consistent basis. For stocks under the category 'B' (defined above), we consider the total number of consecutive years of dividends paid rather than the number of years of dividend growth.
  • Payout ratio: This indicates how comfortably the company can pay the dividend from its earnings. We prefer this ratio to be as low as possible, which would indicate the company's ability to grow the dividend in the future. This ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount per share by the EPS (earnings per share). The cash-flow payout ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount paid per share by the cash flow generated per share.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth: Even though it's the dividend growth rate from the past, this does indicate how fast the company has been able to grow its earnings and dividends in the recent past. The recent past is the best indicator that we have to know what to expect in the next few years.
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth): As the earnings of a company grow, more than likely, dividends will grow accordingly. We will take into account the actual EPS growth of the previous five years and the estimated EPS growth for the next five years. We will add the two numbers and assign weights.
  • Chowder number: So, what's the Chowder number? This number has been named after well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/equity ratio: This ratio will tell us about the company's debt load in relation to its equity. We all know that too much debt can lead to major problems, even for well-known companies. The lower this ratio, the better it is. Sometimes, we find this ratio to be negative or unavailable, even for well-known companies. This can happen for a myriad of reasons and is not always a reason for concern. This is why we use this ratio in combination with the debt/asset ratio (covered next).
  • Debt/asset ratio: This ratio will tell us about the debt load in relation to the company's total assets. In almost all cases, this ratio would be lower than the debt/equity ratio. Also, this ratio is important because, for some companies, the debt/equity ratio is not a reliable indicator.
  • S&P's credit rating: This is the credit rating assigned by the rating agency S&P Global and is indicative of the company's ability to service its debt. This rating can be obtained from the S&P website.
  • PEG ratio: This is also called the price/earnings-to-growth ratio. The PEG ratio is considered to be an indicator if the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly priced. A lower PEG may indicate that a stock is undervalued. However, PEG for a company may differ significantly from one reported source to another, depending on which growth estimate is used in the calculation. Some use past growth, while others may use future expected growth.
  • Distance from 52-week high: We want to select companies that are good, solid companies but also are trading at cheaper valuations currently. They may be cheaper due to some temporary down cycle or some combination of bad news or simply having a bad quarter. This criterion will help bring such companies (with a cheaper valuation) near the top as long as they excel in other criteria as well. This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • Sales or Revenue growth: This is the average growth rate in annual sales or revenue of the company over the last five years. A company can only grow its earnings power as long as it can grow its revenue. Sure, it can grow the earnings by cutting costs, but that can't go on forever.

Selection Of The Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 440 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-export_-_5_Secure_and_Cheap_DGI_-_Apr_2025.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400 plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: Not all companies have a 10-year streak of dividend growth, but many do. We will sort our list based on 10-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ADP, BAH, CI, DOW, EOG, IMO, MPWR, MSFT (8 duplicates).

After removing seven duplicates, we are left with 62 (70-8) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than < 2% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 10-year) was less than 12%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove GWW, WMT, and LIN. After removing them, we are left with 59 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment.

For this reason, we remove APH, MPWR, MCHP, AMAT, QCOM, PHM, WMS, DLB, ZION, SF, BEN, ZURVY, PH, CTAS, CI, CTRA, ET, DG, and VST.

After we remove these 19 names, we are left with 40 names.

The top names from each sector/industry segment are presented:

Financial Services, Banking, and Insurance:

Banks- Regional: (CFR)

Banks- Major: (JPM)

Financial Services - Others: (ARES), (MS), (ARCC)

Security & Exchanges –

Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 50 (or 49 this time), we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.55%, which is nearly 2.5x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is nearly 20 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of 'A-' or higher, while the fifth one has an investment grade rating. If you must need higher dividends, consider B-List or C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details as that will make the article unduly long.

V (Visa):

Visa and Mastercard (MA) are two businesses that operate like a duopoly in the payment processing industry, almost globally. There are other players, but they have a long way to go to catch up with these two titans. They both command expensive valuations in terms of P/E (price to earnings), but Visa trades comparatively cheaper than Mastercard. In terms of dividend yield, there is nothing to brag about, as it is only 0.7%. However, the 5-year and 10-year dividend growth rate is excellent at 15.42% and 17.74%, respectively, and it will be good to include Visa in a group of five stocks to elevate the growth profile of the group.

CVX (CHEVRON):

Chevron is one of the two largest integrated energy companies in the U.S. (the other being Exxon Mobil – "XOM"). Obviously, these two companies need no introduction. Both companies are equally attractive, but we have selected CVX because of its slightly better dividend yield. We are including CVX in the A-list for several reasons, including medium to high dividend yield, a low-cost energy play for the long term, and an excellent credit rating.

The price of oil has been hovering around $70 a barrel, which is a far cry from peak prices in 2022. Even then, it is enough for companies like Chevron to be highly profitable, with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s.

The share price is very close to its 52-week high, but still yields 4.2% in dividends. The payout ratio is in the low 60s on an EPS basis and only in the 40s on a cash flow basis. The five-year dividend growth has been reasonable at 6.5% for an oil company. However, a starting yield of 4.2% with a 6.5%+ annual growth would yield 8% in 10-12 years on a cost basis.

Besides, the company regularly buys back a lot of its own shares, which helps its EPS to grow. This is also apparent from the fact that the outstanding shares are much lower than they were five years ago. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of 'AA' from S&P, which shows its financial strength. We think the company is a good long-term investment for income and dividend investors for relatively safe dividends for many years to come. Long-term investors should make use of any dips in the share price to accumulate it for the long term.

ACN (Accenture)

Accenture is a leading global company in IT consulting and Business services headquartered out of Dublin, Ireland. We are including Accenture in the A-List for its potential as a growth candidate from the technology services sector and for its growing dividends. The other candidate that we have included for growth in the A-list is Visa.

As Accenture likes to describe itself on its website: "Accenture is a leading global professional services company that helps the world's leading businesses, governments and other organizations build their digital core, optimize their operations, accelerate revenue growth and enhance citizen services—creating tangible value at speed and scale. We are a talent- and innovation-led company with approximately 801,000 people serving clients in more than 120 countries. Technology is at the core of change today, and we are one of the world's leaders in helping drive that change, with strong ecosystem relationships. We combine our strength in technology and leadership in cloud, data and AI with unmatched industry experience, functional expertise and global delivery capability."

Accenture is focused on IT consulting and Business services and competes with the likes of International Business Machines (IBM), Infosys (INFY), Cognizant Technology (CTSH), and Capgemini SE (OTCPK:CAPMF). The company has compounded the investors' wealth at an astounding (annualized) rate of over 16% since 2003, compared to 11% from the S&P500. The current dividend yield is decent at nearly 2% from a technology company, and the dividend has grown at a fast clip during the last five years. It also increased the dividends by nearly 15% late last year.

There are obviously some headwinds and risks going forward, especially the uncertainties in the global economy and likely budget cuts in the U.S. federal government contracts (which account for 8% of its revenues). That is one reason that the share price has cratered recently over 20%. However, with the size, scale, and ability of Accenture to adjust, as well as due to the tailwind of AI-related spending, the company is likely to continue to do well. It remains one of the most reputed companies in the IT consulting and Services business. It serves 75% of the global top 500 companies.

MRK (Merck & Co.)

Merck, as a leading pharma company, has an extensive lineup of high-margin drugs with relatively strong patent protection. In addition, it supports a pipeline of new drugs, some of which should come to the market and should ensure strong returns on invested capital over the long term. Even though Merck has suffered some patent losses, new products continue to mitigate the competition from generic drugs.

The most recent quarterly results, which were announced in Feb. 2025, were solid, beating the consensus estimates. Its oncology franchise posted sales of $8.73 billion, showing an uptick of nearly 20% from 2023. Sales of its leading drug, Keytruda, grew by 18.6% year-on-year. Merck's total revenue for the 4th quarter of 2024 was about $15.62 billion, which was less than the previous quarter but still up 6.8% year over year, again beating Wall Street's consensus estimates.

For dividend investors, Merck offers a relatively high yield of 3.7% that looks reasonably secure by its cash flow. Also, the share price is relatively low, considering the fact that they are trading 33% below the 52-week peak. In fact, it is trading nearly 20% below the fair-value estimates by Morningstar. It also regularly buys its own shares to return more money to the shareholders, and recently allocated an additional $10 billion for a share repurchase program.

RIO (Rio Tinto):

Or

[ LYB (LyondellBasell Industries, N.V.) / DOW (Dow Inc)]:

As such, the materials sector is underrepresented in the S&P500, with only about 2% weighting as there are very few large companies in the sector. However, this month, three companies from the material sector are getting highlighted in our ranking system. Last month, we covered LyondellBasell and Dow Inc. Both LYB and DOW remain attractive with very enticing yields. In fact, they are even cheaper compared to the previous month due to the broader market correction.

However, we are including RIO this month in all our three categories (A-list, B-list, and C-list), as this is also trading at attractive levels and yields.

Rio Tinto is one of the leading mining companies in the world. They are the world's largest miners in terms of mining operations in iron ore, copper, aluminum, and other minerals. Since the company is in the Commodity business, the demand for its products is tied to the global economic environment, especially the demand from China. China, being the world's factory, accounts for nearly 60% of RIO's sales, which is also one of the risks with the company.

Most of Rio Tinto's mines are located in safe geographic locations or countries, such as Australia and North America. The company has a large portfolio of mines with low operating costs and has the ability to stay profitable during the commodity cycles. The company also boasts of a strong balance sheet and regularly returns a lot of cash to its shareholders.

For dividend investors, the current price is attractive and supports a yield of 7.3%. However, the dividend payout, paid semi-annually, is variable and can change based on many factors. The current share price is nearly 20% below the fair value assigned by Morningstar and 17% below the 52-week-high.

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield for the group of 5.54%, an average of 21-plus years of dividend history.

PFE (Pfizer):

We are replacing Merck & Co. with Pfizer in our B-List and C-List this month. The reason is obviously the higher yield with slightly higher risk. The health and pharma sector is currently undervalued, but PFE appears to be deeply undervalued. Pfizer's pipeline has been improving with several successful recent drug launches. There are no major patent losses on the horizon until 2028. In addition, Pfizer holds a strong financial position with a sizable cash flow. The 2023 acquisition of Seagen added to Pfizer's overall debt, but the net debt/EBITDA is likely to fall below 2x again by the end of 2025, as the company seems to be focused on paying down the debt.

On the dividend front, the current yield is high, but there is not much growth to be expected. It recently raised the quarterly dividend by a penny, which is in line with the increases during the last 5 years. It amounts to only about a 2.38% increase but still is an increase. Share prices are more than 50% below their peak in 2022, and they still remain very close to their low point in 2024. Since shares were already trading at a low point, they have held much better during the current market correction. Right now, it is ranked as a buy by both the Wall Street and average SA Analysts' ratings. Further, the current price is nearly 40% below Morningstar's fair value estimate.

ARCC (Ares Capital):

Ares Capital is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset values. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest rates environments.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers an attractive yield of roughly 8.6%. Based on core EPS, its payout ratio remains close to 80% for the last 12 months, which is quite reasonable for a BDC.

MPLX (MPLX LP):

For the C-List, we are replacing Chevron with MPLX for the energy sector. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

MPLX is a diversified midstream energy company structured as a master limited partnership. It was formed in 2012 by Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC) for the purpose of owning, operating, acquiring, and developing midstream energy infrastructure assets. To date, MPC remains the largest unitholder of MPLX.

MPLX is a very stable and reliable dividend payer in the mid-stream sector. The current dividend yield is roughly 7.15%. The share price has appreciated quite a bit during the last year, and that has caused the dividend yield to come down, in spite of the yearly increase of 12.5% in Nov. 2024. Even then, it is still higher than other well-established players in the energy MLP sector, such as Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD) and Energy Transfer LP (ET). The company has paid and consecutively raised the payout every year since its inception in 2012.

The company maintains a solid balance sheet with an investor-grade BBB credit rating, and its dividend payouts are well covered. On the negative side, with so much price appreciation in the rearview mirror, future growth may not match the growth of the last 10 years. That said, this is purely an income investment, and we can expect a very stable dividend of roughly 7% plus for the foreseeable future, even if the growth is tepid in the next few years.

BTI (British American Tobacco):

Some investors may not like Tobacco stocks, and that is understandable. But if you are okay with investing in tobacco stocks, BTI is a good choice for dividend and income investors. The share price has already gained nearly 30% from its lows in 2024, so the opportunity of getting it at the super low price of under $30 a share is long gone. Still, at the current prices, the yield is still very attractive and is close to 8%. The company has excelled in growing the new categories of products compared to some of its peers. At current valuations, this is mostly an income investment; however, a 2-3% appreciation over and above the income is quite possible. In the short and medium term, the dividend payouts appear to be reasonably safe; however, there are no guarantees.

Final C-LIST Takeaway:

Apparently, this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up as much as 7.58%. However, this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but the yields are very attractive. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. That said, it's a highly diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.
